description: Azure Well-Architected Framework rules evaluated against the Ranger audit manifest. Rules do not require re-collection — they evaluate from a saved manifest. v2.2.0 (#236) adds structured `remediation` blocks per rule (rationale, steps, sample PowerShell, effort, impact, docsUrl) to support the WAF Compliance Roadmap (#241), Gap-to-Goal projection (#242), per-pillar checklist (#238), and Get-RangerRemediation (#243).

weighting.description: Each rule carries weight 1-3. Warning states award 0.5 x weight. Fail awards 0. Pass awards full weight x (awardedPoints/maxPoints).

prioritization.description: v2.2.0 (#241): priorityScore = (weight * severityMultiplier * impactFactor) / effortFactor. Used by WAF Compliance Roadmap to bucket failing rules into Now/Next/Later tiers.

rules.title: Cluster nodes are healthy
rules.description: One or more cluster nodes are in an unhealthy state, which may impact workload availability and resiliency.
rules.recommendation: Investigate unhealthy cluster nodes via Get-ClusterNode and resolve faults before declaring the environment production-ready.

remediation.rationale: A cluster in Down or Paused state cannot host workloads reliably. Unhealthy nodes degrade quorum and resiliency, and may drop VMs during the next failure event.
steps: Run Get-ClusterNode on any healthy node to confirm which node(s) report Down/Paused
Check the event log on the unhealthy node: Get-WinEvent -LogName System -MaxEvents 200
Resolve hardware faults (power, network, storage) or patch state, then resume the node: Resume-ClusterNode -Name <NodeName>
Re-run Ranger to verify healthSummary.unhealthy == 0
remediation.samplePowerShell:
Get-ClusterNode -Cluster $ClusterName | Where-Object State -ne 'Up'
# For each unhealthy node:
# Resume-ClusterNode -Cluster $ClusterName -Name <NodeName>

rules.title: No unhealthy storage disks
rules.description: Unhealthy physical disks reduce storage resiliency and increase the risk of data loss under a second failure.
rules.recommendation: Review disk health via Get-PhysicalDisk. Replace failed or warning-state disks and verify storage pool repair completes before handoff.

remediation.rationale: S2D tolerates N-1 disk failures per fault domain; an unhealthy disk consumes that tolerance. A second failure before repair completes can cause data loss.
steps: List unhealthy disks: Get-PhysicalDisk | Where-Object HealthStatus -ne 'Healthy'
Check SMART/operational status for each: Get-PhysicalDisk -UniqueId <id> | Select-Object *
Retire the failed disk: Set-PhysicalDisk -UniqueId <id> -Usage Retired
Physically replace, then add the new disk: Get-PhysicalDisk -CanPool $true | Add-PhysicalDisk -StoragePool <poolName>
Monitor repair: Get-StorageJob | Where-Object Name -like 'Repair*'
remediation.samplePowerShell: Get-PhysicalDisk -CimSession $ClusterName | Where-Object HealthStatus -ne 'Healthy' | Format-Table FriendlyName, SerialNumber, HealthStatus, OperationalStatus

rules.title: Fault domains are configured
rules.description: Fault domain configuration ensures workload placement accounts for physical failure boundaries. An empty fault domain set means workloads may not be distributed across failure boundaries.
rules.recommendation: Configure fault domains using New-ClusterFaultDomain to ensure VMs are distributed across physical racks or chassis.

remediation.rationale: Without explicit fault domains, Failover Cluster places VMs and storage copies without awareness of racks/chassis. A rack PDU failure can take down correlated copies.
steps: Document the physical layout: site -> rack -> chassis -> node
Create fault domains top-down: New-ClusterFaultDomain -Type Site -Name Primary
Add rack and chassis levels as needed, then assign nodes: Set-ClusterFaultDomain -Name Node01 -Parent Rack01
Verify: Get-ClusterFaultDomain

rules.title: Cluster quorum is configured
rules.description: Without a recognised quorum configuration, the cluster may not survive a node failure.
rules.recommendation: Verify cluster quorum configuration via Get-ClusterQuorum. A witness (cloud or disk) is strongly recommended for even-node clusters.

remediation.rationale: Quorum determines how many nodes must agree for the cluster to remain online. An even-node cluster with no witness can split-brain or go offline on a single failure.
steps: Inspect current quorum: Get-ClusterQuorum
For 2-4 node clusters, configure a Cloud Witness backed by a storage account: Set-ClusterQuorum -CloudWitness -AccountName <name> -AccessKey <key>
Verify the witness is Online in Failover Cluster Manager
Document the witness configuration in the environment runbook

rules.title: No unhealthy storage pools
rules.description: A degraded storage pool reduces redundancy and may cause data loss if additional disks fail.
rules.recommendation: Review pool health via Get-StoragePool. Trigger repair jobs and monitor until pools return to Healthy state.

remediation.rationale: A degraded pool has lost a copy of some data slabs. Workloads stay online but a second failure in the same fault domain will cause data loss.
steps: List pool health: Get-StoragePool | Select-Object FriendlyName, HealthStatus, OperationalStatus
Inspect active repair jobs: Get-StorageJob
If no repair is running, trigger one: Repair-VirtualDisk -FriendlyName <vdName>
Address the root cause disk failure (see REL-002) in parallel

rules.title: Backup items are tracked for workloads
rules.description: No backup items were discovered in the scoped resource group. Workloads without backup coverage are at risk of data loss in a disaster scenario.
rules.recommendation: Configure Azure Backup for VMs via Azure Site Recovery or Azure Backup vaults, then confirm items appear in the resource group.

remediation.rationale: Ransomware, accidental deletion, and site loss are not mitigated by S2D resiliency alone. Backup provides the last line of defence and satisfies most regulatory controls.
steps: Create a Recovery Services vault in Azure in the same region as the Arc VMs
Enable Azure Backup for Azure Local Arc VMs (preview/GA — check current docs)
Configure backup policies: daily RPO, 30-day short retention, monthly for 12 months
Run a test restore after first successful backup
Verify: az backup item list --resource-group $ResourceGroup --vault-name $vault

rules.title: Logical networks are configured
rules.description: Azure Local logical networks define the Arc VM networking fabric (subnets, VLANs, IP pools). Without them Arc VM provisioning will fail.
rules.recommendation: Create at least one logical network with subnet definitions via the Azure Local portal (Networking > Logical networks) or via Arc Resource Bridge.

remediation.rationale: Arc VMs require a Microsoft.AzureStackHCI/logicalNetworks resource to bind to. Without one, every VM provisioning attempt fails at network assignment.
steps: In the Azure portal, open the Azure Local cluster resource
Navigate to Networking > Logical networks > Create
Supply VLAN ID, subnet CIDR, IP pool start/end, DNS and gateway
Verify the resource appears under the cluster's custom location

rules.title: Arc Resource Bridge is deployed and connected
rules.description: Arc Resource Bridge is the on-premises control plane for Arc-enabled services. Its absence or non-connected state blocks Arc VM provisioning and management.
rules.recommendation: Deploy Arc Resource Bridge via Azure Local portal (Arc services > Resource Bridge) and confirm it reports Connected.

remediation.rationale: Arc Resource Bridge projects Azure APIs (logicalNetworks, customLocations, virtualMachineInstances) onto the cluster. Without it, Arc VMs cannot be provisioned or managed from Azure.
steps: Ensure outbound HTTPS to required Azure endpoints is permitted (see docs for the allow-list)
In the Azure Local portal, run the Arc services deployment wizard
Provide subscription, resource group, region, and Custom Location name
Wait 30-60 minutes for deployment to complete
Confirm ConnectedClusters and appliances resource shows 'Running'/'Connected'

rules.title: VMs are distributed evenly across cluster nodes
rules.description: Uneven VM distribution overloads some nodes while leaving others underused. High coefficient of variation (>0.3) signals an imbalanced cluster.
rules.recommendation: Rebalance VMs across nodes using Live Migration. Consider enabling Cluster Balancer (CauClusterAware) to automate placement.

remediation.rationale: Uneven VM density concentrates failure impact and memory/CPU contention on hot nodes. A balanced cluster tolerates node loss with less performance cliff.
steps: Identify hot and cold nodes from the VM Distribution section of this report
Live-migrate VMs from hot to cold: Move-ClusterVirtualMachineRole -Name <vm> -Node <targetNode>
Consider Failover Cluster's built-in balancer: (Get-Cluster).AutoBalancerMode = 2 (Always)
Re-run Ranger to confirm CV < 0.3

rules.title: Secured-Core is enabled on cluster nodes
rules.description: Secured-Core provides hardware-anchored security capabilities including DRTM, HVCI, and Credential Guard. Nodes without Secured-Core enabled have a reduced security baseline.
rules.recommendation: Enable Secured-Core on all cluster nodes via the Azure Local portal or PowerShell. Verify UEFI Secure Boot and TPM 2.0 are active on each node.

remediation.rationale: Secured-Core anchors HVCI, DRTM, and Credential Guard in hardware. Without it, credential-theft and rootkit defences lack their hardware root-of-trust.
steps: Confirm UEFI Secure Boot and TPM 2.0 are enabled in BIOS/iDRAC/iLO on every node
In the Azure Local portal, open the cluster resource -> Security -> Secured-Core
Enable and wait ~5 minutes per node for configuration to apply
Re-run Ranger to confirm securedCoreNodes == nodeCount

rules.title: BitLocker encryption is enabled
rules.description: BitLocker at-rest encryption was not confirmed on cluster volumes. Data at rest may be accessible if storage media is removed.
rules.recommendation: Enable BitLocker on Cluster Shared Volumes using Enable-AzureStackHCIBitLocker or via the Azure Local portal.

remediation.rationale: Encryption at rest is a baseline control for most regulated workloads. Disks pulled from a decommissioned node are otherwise readable.
steps: Confirm TPM 2.0 is enabled on every node (prerequisite for BitLocker)
Enable via the Azure Local portal: cluster -> Security -> Data protection -> Enable encryption
Store recovery keys in Active Directory or Azure Key Vault
Verify per-volume: manage-bde -status

rules.title: WDAC application control policy is present
rules.description: Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) was not detected on cluster nodes. Without WDAC, unauthorised code may run on cluster hosts.
rules.recommendation: Deploy a WDAC policy via the Azure Local portal (Security > Application control). Use Microsoft's recommended block list as a baseline.

remediation.rationale: WDAC blocks unsigned and unauthorised binaries at kernel load time. The default Microsoft recommended block list stops most commodity LOLBin abuse.
steps: Deploy the Microsoft recommended WDAC block policy in audit mode first
Review the 3001 event log on each node for 7 days to find legitimate blocked apps
Switch the policy to enforced mode after adding allow rules for legitimate tools
Maintain a signed policy supplier process for ongoing updates
remediation.samplePowerShell:
# Download and deploy the Microsoft recommended block list:
# https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/application-security/application-control/windows-defender-application-control/design/microsoft-recommended-block-rules

rules.title: No certificates expiring within 90 days
rules.description: One or more tracked certificates are within 90 days of expiry. Expired certificates can cause service outages and break Arc connectivity.
rules.recommendation: Review certificates approaching expiry using the identitySecurity manifest domain. Renew or rotate certificates before expiry to prevent service disruption.

remediation.rationale: Expired Arc/cluster certificates silently break Azure connectivity, monitoring pipelines, and management plane access. Renewal requires lead time — don't leave it to the last week.
steps: From the Identity & Security report section, list the expiring certificates with subject and thumbprint
For cluster-internal certs, run the Azure Local certificate rotation: Start-AzStackHciCertificateRotation (if applicable)
For Arc agent certs, rotate with: azcmagent reconnect
Document the renewal date and calendar the next rotation

rules.title: Defender for Cloud is enabled
rules.description: Microsoft Defender for Cloud was not confirmed active for this environment. Defender for Cloud provides threat detection, security posture management, and vulnerability assessment.
rules.recommendation: Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud via the Azure portal for the subscription hosting the Azure Local cluster resources.

remediation.rationale: Defender for Cloud + Defender for Servers provides the threat detection, vulnerability assessment, and file integrity monitoring baseline that most Azure Local workloads require for regulatory compliance.
steps: Enable Defender for Cloud on the subscription hosting the cluster resource
Enable the Defender for Servers Plan 2 for Arc-connected machines
Confirm the security policy assignment applies to the cluster RG
Wait 24 hours for initial vulnerability scans to complete

rules.title: Azure Policy assignments are present
rules.description: No Azure Policy assignments were found in the scoped resource group. Policy assignments enforce guardrails and compliance requirements across Azure Local resources.
rules.recommendation: Assign appropriate Azure Policy initiatives (e.g. Azure Security Benchmark, CIS Azure Local) to the subscription or resource group.

remediation.rationale: Policy assignments provide drift detection and preventive guardrails. Without any assignments, nothing flags a resource that drifts from baseline.
steps: Navigate to Azure Portal -> Policy -> Definitions
Assign the Azure Security Benchmark initiative to the subscription
Assign the Azure Local Specific initiative (or equivalent) to the cluster RG
Review the compliance summary after 24 hours and remediate any non-compliant resources

rules.title: Arc monitoring agents deployed on all nodes
rules.description: Azure Monitor Agent (AMA) must be present and Succeeded on every Arc-enrolled node for HCI Insights, security logs, and Defender for Servers coverage.
rules.passMessage: Azure Monitor Agent present on 100% of nodes.
rules.warningMessage: AMA installed on {value}% of nodes — some nodes missing.
rules.failMessage: AMA not detected on any node ({value}%).
rules.recommendation: Deploy Azure Monitor Agent to every Arc-connected node via Azure Local portal (Monitoring > Insights) or Arc extension management.

remediation.rationale: AMA is the single agent path for HCI Insights, security event ingestion, and Defender for Servers. Coverage gaps create blind spots for incident response.
steps: Identify nodes missing AMA from the Arc Extensions section of this report

Install AMA via Arc extension: Set-AzConnectedMachineExtension -Name AzureMonitorWindowsAgent -ExtensionType AzureMonitorWindowsAgent -Publisher Microsoft.Azure.Monitor -MachineName $node -ResourceGroupName $rg -Location $region
Associate the machines with a DCR (see OE-001)
Confirm extension provisioningState == Succeeded for every node

rules.title: Nodes running matching Arc agent versions
rules.description: Agent version drift across nodes indicates inconsistent patch state and is a common source of support issues.
rules.recommendation: Bring all nodes onto the same Arc agent version via Update-AzConnectedMachineExtension or Azure Local portal.

remediation.rationale: Mixed agent versions across nodes cause inconsistent extension behaviour and complicate root-cause analysis. Fixing drift is usually a five-minute bulk update.
steps: List the current agent version on each node from the Cluster Nodes section of this report
Update the out-of-date nodes: azcmagent upgrade (per node) or automate via Arc extension updates
Re-run Ranger to confirm agentVersionDrift.uniqueVersions == 1

rules.title: ESU-eligible VMs are enrolled in Extended Security Updates
rules.description: One or more VMs running end-of-support Windows Server versions are eligible for free Extended Security Updates via Arc but are not yet enrolled.
rules.recommendation: Enroll eligible Arc-connected VMs in Extended Security Updates via the Azure portal (Arc > Extended Security Updates). No licensing cost applies for Arc-enrolled VMs on Azure Local.

remediation.rationale: ESU enrolment is free for Arc-connected VMs running on Azure Local. Unenrolled eligible VMs drift off security support with no financial reason.
steps: Identify eligible-but-not-enrolled VMs from the Cost & Licensing section
In the Azure portal, open Arc > Extended Security Updates > Create ESU license
Link the license to the Arc-connected machines via keyProfile
Confirm each VM shows ESU status Entitled
remediation.samplePowerShell:
# ESU management is portal-driven; inspect current state via Az.Resources:
Get-AzResource -ResourceType 'Microsoft.HybridCompute/licenses'

rules.title: Azure subscription context is configured
rules.description: No Azure subscription was associated with this discovery run. Without subscription context, cost posture, policy coverage, and Azure-billed services cannot be assessed.
rules.recommendation: Ensure the Ranger config includes a valid subscriptionId and resourceGroup matching the Azure Local registration to enable full cost and policy assessment.

remediation.rationale: Without subscriptionId set in config, Ranger cannot fetch Advisor recommendations, policy assignments, cost data, or Arc extension state. Scoring blind-spots follow.
steps: Edit the Ranger configuration (config/config.json or -ConfigPath) and set targets.azure.subscriptionId and targets.azure.resourceGroup
Re-run Invoke-AzureLocalRanger with the updated config

rules.title: Azure Hybrid Benefit (AHB) adoption
rules.description: AHB for Azure Local reduces per-core billing by applying qualifying Windows Server licenses. High adoption directly reduces monthly Azure Local costs.
rules.passMessage: AHB adoption at {value}% — maximum savings captured.
rules.warningMessage: AHB adoption at {value}% — additional cores eligible but not enrolled.
rules.failMessage: AHB adoption only {value}% — significant unleveraged licensing savings.
rules.recommendation: Enroll qualifying Software Assurance Windows Server licenses into Azure Hybrid Benefit for Azure Local via the cluster resource in the Azure portal.

remediation.rationale: AHB applies qualifying on-prem Windows Server licenses against Azure Local per-core billing. Every unenrolled core is direct monthly spend with zero compensating control.
steps: Confirm qualifying Windows Server Datacenter licenses with Software Assurance are available
In the Azure portal, open the Azure Local cluster resource -> Configuration -> Software Assurance
Enable Azure Hybrid Benefit and declare the qualifying cores
Verify the resource property softwareAssuranceProperties.softwareAssuranceIntent == 'Enable'

rules.title: Data Collection Rules are configured for monitoring
rules.description: No Data Collection Rules (DCRs) were found in the scoped resource group. DCRs are required to route Azure Monitor telemetry from cluster nodes and Arc machines to a Log Analytics workspace.
rules.recommendation: Create Data Collection Rules in the Azure portal targeting the cluster's Arc-connected machines. Associate rules with the Log Analytics workspace used for HCI Insights.

remediation.rationale: Without a DCR, AMA-collected events and metrics have no destination. Monitoring dashboards and alerts silently stay empty.
steps: In the Azure portal, open Monitor -> Data Collection Rules -> Create
Select the platform type Windows and add the cluster's Arc machines as resources
Add data sources: Windows Event Logs (System, Application, Security), Performance Counters
Set the destination as the Log Analytics workspace used by HCI Insights
Associate the DCR with the Arc machines via a DCR Association
remediation.samplePowerShell:
# Create a minimal DCR for Windows Event logs:
New-AzDataCollectionRule -ResourceGroupName $ResourceGroup -Location $Region -RuleName 'dcr-azlocal' -DataFlow @{ Streams = @('Microsoft-Event'); Destinations = @($workspaceName) }

rules.title: Azure Monitor alert rules are defined
rules.description: No Azure Monitor alert rules were found in the scoped resource group. Without alert rules, operational incidents may go undetected until reported by users.
rules.recommendation: Define metric and log-based alert rules for key Azure Local health signals: cluster health faults, storage capacity threshold, node CPU and memory pressure.

remediation.rationale: Alerts are the difference between proactive ops and incident reports via user complaints. Baseline alerts cover the 90% of cluster faults that predict downtime.
steps: Create log-based alerts in Log Analytics for cluster health events (FaultDomain, HealthFault)
Create metric-based alerts for node CPU > 85% for 15 minutes, memory < 10% free, storage utilisation > 80%
Route alerts to an Action Group (email/Teams/PagerDuty)
Test each alert with a synthetic condition before declaring ops-ready

rules.title: Update maintenance configurations are defined
rules.description: No Azure Update Manager maintenance configurations were found. Without scheduled maintenance windows, update compliance cannot be enforced and emergency patching may be the only path.
rules.recommendation: Create maintenance configurations in Azure Update Manager to schedule regular update windows for the cluster nodes.

remediation.rationale: Without a maintenance configuration, update cadence is tribal knowledge. Update Manager provides auditable, scheduled patching aligned with change windows.
steps: Create a maintenance configuration in Azure Portal -> Update Manager -> Maintenance configurations
Set scope to the cluster's RG with recurrence (e.g. 2nd Tuesday 22:00-02:00)
Assign the configuration to the Arc-connected machines
Review compliance weekly

rules.title: Azure Monitor Agent is deployed on cluster nodes
rules.description: No cluster nodes were found with Azure Monitor Agent installed. AMA is required for HCI Insights, DCR-based telemetry, and health monitoring from the Azure portal.
rules.recommendation: Deploy Azure Monitor Agent to all cluster nodes via the Azure Local portal (Monitoring > Insights) or via Arc extension management.

remediation.rationale: AMA is the prerequisite agent for every Azure Monitor scenario on Azure Local. Missing AMA means no HCI Insights, no DCR-fed logs, no Defender for Servers telemetry.
steps: See SEC-007 — same remediation applies to all monitoring scenarios

rules.title: HCI Insights is enabled in Log Analytics
rules.description: HCI Insights was not detected in any associated Log Analytics workspace. HCI Insights provides the primary monitoring dashboard for Azure Local environments in the Azure portal.
rules.recommendation: Enable HCI Insights from the Azure Local cluster resource in the Azure portal under Monitoring > Insights.

remediation.rationale: HCI Insights is the primary portal dashboard for cluster health, VM telemetry, and trend analysis. Without it, operators rely on cmdlet scraping or third-party tooling.
steps: Navigate to the Azure Local cluster resource in Azure Portal
Open Monitoring -> Insights -> Configure
Select the Log Analytics workspace (same workspace used for DCRs in OE-001)
Wait ~15 minutes for the initial data population
remediation.samplePowerShell:
# HCI Insights enablement is portal-driven.
# Verify the workspace association:
Get-AzOperationalInsightsWorkspace -ResourceGroupName $ResourceGroup

rules.title: Manifest schema validation passed
rules.description: The collection manifest failed schema validation. This may indicate incomplete collection or a version mismatch between the collector and schema contract.
rules.recommendation: Review manifest schema validation warnings in run.schemaValidation. Re-run collection if the issue persists or file a bug at github.com/AzureLocal/azurelocal-ranger/issues.

remediation.rationale: Schema validation failure means collected data may not flow through downstream analysis correctly. It often indicates a collector error or partial data.
steps: Review the run.schemaValidation object in the manifest for specific validation messages
Check the run.log for collector errors or warnings
Re-run Invoke-AzureLocalRanger with -Verbose to diagnose
If the issue persists, file a bug report with the manifest validation messages

rules.title: Storage paths configured for Arc VMs
rules.description: Arc VM provisioning requires at least one Microsoft.AzureStackHCI/storageContainers resource (storage path) to host virtual disks.
rules.recommendation: Create at least one storage path in the Azure Local portal (Storage > Storage paths) pointing at a Cluster Shared Volume.

remediation.rationale: Storage paths project a CSV path as an Arc-managed resource so Arc VMs can use it for virtual disk storage. No storage path = no Arc VM provisioning.
steps: In the Azure Local portal, open the cluster resource -> Storage > Storage paths -> Create
Select a Cluster Shared Volume and supply a name
Verify the resource appears as Microsoft.AzureStackHCI/storageContainers

rules.title: Custom location configured
rules.description: A Custom Location projects Azure services onto the cluster via the Arc Resource Bridge. Missing custom locations block Arc VM and AKS Arc workloads.
rules.recommendation: Create a custom location linked to the Arc Resource Bridge via Azure Local portal (Arc services > Custom locations).

remediation.rationale: Custom Locations are the resource targeting mechanism for Arc services (Arc VM, AKS Arc, Arc-enabled SQL MI). Without one, Arc workloads have nowhere to deploy.
steps: Deploy Arc Resource Bridge first (REL-008)
In Azure Portal -> Custom locations -> Create, select the appliance and target RG
Enable the resource providers (Microsoft.AzureStackHCI) for the custom location
Verify the resource appears as Microsoft.ExtendedLocation/customLocations
remediation.samplePowerShell:
# Created during Arc Resource Bridge deployment; verify state:
Get-AzResource -ResourceType 'Microsoft.ExtendedLocation/customLocations'

rules.title: Images sourced from compute gallery or marketplace
rules.description: Marketplace and compute-gallery images carry tracked version, publisher, and SKU metadata — essential for patch management and compliance auditing. Manually uploaded VHDs have no tracked provenance.
rules.recommendation: Deploy Arc VMs from the Azure Local marketplace or a managed compute gallery rather than custom-uploaded VHDs.

remediation.rationale: Tracked images give you provenance, patch level, and publisher signatures. Custom VHDs become long-lived snowflakes that no-one remembers how to rebuild.
steps: In the Azure Local portal, open the cluster -> Images -> Add image from marketplace
Pick a supported Windows Server or Linux SKU and download
Standardise new Arc VM deployments on the marketplace image
Plan a remediation window to rebuild existing VMs from tracked images

rules.title: No cluster nodes are at high CPU utilization
rules.description: One or more cluster nodes are running at sustained high CPU utilization. This may indicate workload density is too high or that the cluster is approaching compute capacity limits.
rules.recommendation: Review per-node CPU utilization in the performance domain. Consider migrating high-density VMs or adding nodes to increase headroom.

remediation.rationale: Sustained CPU > 85% leaves no headroom for VM restart storms after a node fails. A cluster that runs hot already is one failure from a performance collapse.
steps: Identify hot nodes from the Performance section of this report
Live-migrate a subset of VMs to cooler nodes: Move-ClusterVirtualMachineRole
Review top consumers via per-VM CPU metrics; rightsize or decommission
Plan for node addition if sustained utilisation remains > 70% after rebalancing

rules.title: Storage utilization is within safe limits
rules.description: Total storage utilization exceeds 80% of usable capacity. Operating above 80% increases rebuild time after disk failure and may trigger performance throttling.
rules.recommendation: Review the Storage Pool Capacity section of this report. Decommission unused VMs or volumes, or expand storage capacity to restore headroom.

remediation.rationale: S2D reserves ~10% for rebuild capacity. Above 80% usable the pool cannot self-heal from a disk failure without manual intervention — a ticking clock.
steps: Identify stale or oversized volumes from the Storage section of this report
Decommission unused VMs: Remove-VM (+ delete associated VHDs)
Shrink over-provisioned dynamic VHDs: Optimize-VHD -Mode Full
If still > 80%, plan storage expansion: add disks to nodes or add nodes

rules.title: Management services are running on cluster nodes
rules.description: No running management services were detected on cluster nodes. Management service availability is required for Azure Local health monitoring, WAC connectivity, and Arc agent operation.
rules.recommendation: Verify that required management services (Health Service, MDAG, Arc agent) are running on all cluster nodes.

remediation.rationale: Key services (Health Service, WMI, Arc agent) underpin all management surfaces. If they are stopped, reports will be empty and Azure portal operations will fail.
steps: On each node, check service state: Get-Service -Name HealthService, ClusSvc, himds
Start any stopped services and set startup type to Automatic
Investigate root cause if services keep stopping (event log, cluster events)
